source: SHRM, BLS Employee Benefits Survey, IRS guidelines

401k_match.name: 401(k) Employer Match
401k_match.whatItIs: Your employer puts free money into your retirement account when you contribute.
401k_match.avgBenefit: Average match: 50% of first 6% of salary = 3% free money
401k_match.valueExample: On $60,000 salary: contributing 6% = $3,600/year from you + $1,800 from employer = $5,400 total. The $1,800 match is FREE money you lose if you don't contribute enough.
401k_match.richyAction: If you're not contributing at least enough to get the full match, you are literally declining free money. This is ALWAYS the first financial priority after basic expenses.

hsa.name: Health Savings Account (HSA)
hsa.whatItIs: Triple tax-advantaged account: tax-deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for medical expenses.
hsa.eligibility: Must have a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)
hsa.avgBenefit: The only account with three tax benefits. At 22% tax bracket, contributing $4,300 saves $946 in taxes alone.
hsa.secretWealth: You can invest HSA funds and let them grow for decades. Pay medical expenses out of pocket now, let HSA grow, withdraw tax-free in retirement. It's a stealth retirement account.
hsa.richyAction: If you have an HDHP, an HSA is the most powerful tax-advantaged account that exists. Contribute the max, invest it, and don't touch it until retirement.

fsa.name: Flexible Spending Account (FSA)
fsa.whatItIs: Pre-tax dollars for medical expenses (Health FSA) or dependent care (Dependent Care FSA).
fsa.keyRule: USE IT OR LOSE IT — money expires at end of plan year (some plans allow $640 rollover or 2.5 month grace period).
fsa.avgBenefit: At 22% tax bracket, $3,300 FSA saves $726 in taxes. Plus you save 7.65% FICA = another $252. Total tax savings: $978.
fsa.richyAction: Calculate your expected medical/dental/vision costs for the year and contribute that amount. Don't over-contribute (use it or lose it) but don't under-contribute either.

dependent_care_fsa.name: Dependent Care FSA
dependent_care_fsa.whatItIs: Pre-tax dollars for childcare, daycare, preschool, before/after school care, and elder care.
dependent_care_fsa.avgBenefit: On $5,000 at 22% bracket: save $1,100 in income tax + $383 in FICA = $1,483 total savings. If you're paying for childcare anyway, this is free money.
dependent_care_fsa.eligibility: Must have a dependent child under 13 or disabled dependent
dependent_care_fsa.richyAction: If you pay for childcare, this is one of the biggest tax savings available. $1,483/year saved on childcare you're already paying for.

commuter_benefits.name: Commuter/Transit Benefits
commuter_benefits.whatItIs: Pre-tax dollars for public transit passes, vanpooling, and parking.
commuter_benefits.avgBenefit: Monthly transit pass of $100 pre-tax saves $264/year in taxes (at 22% bracket)
commuter_benefits.richyAction: If you commute by public transit or pay for parking, you should always use pre-tax dollars. It's automatic savings.

employee_stock_purchase.name: Employee Stock Purchase Plan (ESPP)
employee_stock_purchase.whatItIs: Buy company stock at a 5-15% discount through payroll deductions.
employee_stock_purchase.avgBenefit: 15% instant return on your money. Even if you sell immediately, you pocket the discount (minus taxes). If max contribution is $5,000/period, that's $750 guaranteed profit.
employee_stock_purchase.strategy: Contribute, buy at discount, sell immediately for guaranteed 15% gain. Don't hold unless you want company-specific risk.
employee_stock_purchase.richyAction: If your company offers ESPP with a 15% discount, it's one of the safest guaranteed returns available. Contribute the max and sell if you want to diversify.

tuition_reimbursement.name: Tuition Reimbursement
tuition_reimbursement.whatItIs: Employer pays for courses, degrees, or certifications related to your job.
tuition_reimbursement.avgBenefit: Average: $5,250/year tax-free (IRS Section 127 exclusion). Some employers offer more (taxable above $5,250).
tuition_reimbursement.richyAction: Only 2% of eligible employees use tuition reimbursement. If your employer offers it and you're considering any education or certification, this is thousands of dollars in free career development.

employee_discounts.name: Employee Discounts & Perks
employee_discounts.whatItIs: Negotiated discounts on cell phones, gym memberships, insurance, travel, retail, and more.
commonDiscounts: Cell phone: 15-25% off monthly bill (AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile all offer employer discounts)
Gym membership: corporate rates 20-50% below retail
Travel: corporate rates on hotels, rental cars
employee_discounts.richyAction: Check your company intranet or HR portal for the full list of employee discounts. Most people don't realize their employer has negotiated discounts on cell phones, gym memberships, and insurance.

life_insurance.name: Employer Life Insurance
life_insurance.whatItIs: Most employers provide free basic life insurance (usually 1-2x annual salary).
life_insurance.avgBenefit: Free coverage of $50,000-150,000 depending on salary. Supplemental coverage available at group rates (cheaper than individual).
life_insurance.richyAction: Review your free coverage amount. If you have dependents, you likely need 10-12x your income. Buy supplemental through your employer — group rates are 30-50% cheaper than individual policies.

wellness_programs.name: Wellness Programs & Incentives
wellness_programs.whatItIs: Many employers offer cash incentives, premium discounts, or HSA contributions for completing health activities.
wellness_programs.richyAction: Check if your employer has a wellness program. Many people miss out on $200-1,500/year in easy rewards just for getting an annual checkup and filling out a health questionnaire.

totalValueEstimate: A typical employee with access to all common benefits who fully utilizes them captures $3,000-8,000/year in additional value beyond their salary. Most employees capture less than 40% of available benefits.
